data.title: NGL Energy: Beneficial 2026 Guidance And Undervalued, But With Low Margin Of Safety (Downgrade)
data.description:
NGL Energy Partners LP (NYSE:NGL) recently provided a beneficial 2026 Adjusted EBITDA of about $615-$625 million, which implies a valuation close to 7.3x 2026 Adjusted EBITDA. The company reported the acquisition of shares under its common unit repurchase program, and noted the simplification of its business mix and sale of assets to lower its total net debt. Considering the recent increase in the crude oil price, I redesigned my financial model, and obtained an implied valuation that is significantly higher than the price reported in my previous article. I do see risks from changes in the regulations about GHG Emissions and new regulatory rules imposed by the SEC. With all that being said, I do believe that NGL continues to be undervalued. When I first covered NGL, the stock traded at $3.32. Right now, NGL trades at about $4.5 per share.

Business Model

NGL Energy Partners offers a wide range of services including transportation, blending, and marketing of crude oil as well as water solutions.

The company operates in the Delaware Basin, the DJ Basin in Colorado, and the Eagle Ford Basin in Texas. In the last annual report, NGL reported 569 employees. The largest revenue comes from water transportation. The company appears to operate the largest network of large diameter produced water pipelines in the Northern Delaware Basin. In the last quarterly report, the company noted property and equipment of about $2.066 billion and book value close to $697.4 million. The current market capitalization is not far from $600 million. Given these figures, in my view, most investors would say that NGL appears quite undervalued.

Our core asset in the Water Solutions segment is our system located in the Northern Delaware Basin, where we own and operate the largest integrated network of large diameter produced water pipelines, recycling facilities and disposal wells. Source: 10-k

I recommend readers to have a look at my previous article about the company, where I offered a lot more information about the business model. We made beneficial stock returns thanks to NGL, and I believe that the company could distribute more stock returns in the near future. In this article, I am providing a new financial model, with more assumptions and a new price target.

In the most recent press release, NGL reported 2026 Adjusted EBITDA of about $615-$625 million. If we assume an enterprise value of $4.54 billion, the company appears to be trading at not far from 7.3x 2026 Adjusted EBITDA. The company does not look expensive right now. I took into account the guidance given by NGL to design my financial model.

We are guiding Fiscal 2026 full year consolidated Adjusted EBITDA to a range of $615 -$625 million which is an increase over Fiscal 2025 actuals adjusted for EBITDA associated with asset sales. Also, we are guiding to $45 million in maintenance and $60 million of growth capital expenditures for Fiscal 2026. Source: News Release

Strategic Actions To Accelerate Cash Flow Generation Will Most Likely Enhance The Demand For The Stock

In 2024 and 2025, NGL sold a significant number of assets in order to lower the total amount of debt as well as to invest in core areas in which the NGL has expertise. In this regard, in the last annual report, which was issued a few weeks ago, the management provided the following information. NGL appears to be focusing on offering stable cash flow generation, an improvement of the credit profile, and a simplification of the business mix. With this in mind, I would expect new transactions to accelerate free cash flow in 2025 and 2026. As soon as more investors understand the initiatives of NGL, in my opinion, the demand for the stock will most likely increase.

Over the past several years, we have undertaken a number of important strategic actions in an effort to capitalize on the Partnership’s core areas of competitive strength and focus on generating stable, growing and predictable cash flows, while improving our credit profile. We believe our actions have simplified our business mix and have allowed us to focus on what we believe are the core areas of our business and improved our overall financial position. Source: 10-k

The Common Unit Repurchase Program And Reduction In The Unit Could Enhance The Stock Price

Considering the valuation of the stock, NGL decided to acquire its own shares. In 2024, the company launched a share buyback program worth $50 million that, I believe, could accelerate the demand for the stock. In the year ended March 31, 2025, the company acquired 500,000 units for $2.1 million. It means that the company acquired shares at close to $4.2 per share. Right now, the stock does not trade far from the price mark at which the company bought shares.

On June 5, 2024, the board of directors of our GP authorized a common unit repurchase program, under which we may repurchase up to $50.0 million of our outstanding common units from time to time in the open market or in other privately negotiated transactions. This program does not have a fixed expiration date. The common unit repurchase program authorization does not obligate us to repurchase any dollar amount or number of common units. During the year ended March 31, 2025, we repurchased 500,000 units for an aggregate price of $2.1 million, including commissions. Source: 10-k

Regulations With Regard To GHG Emissions Could Deteriorate Future Net Income Growth

NGL and the oil and gas industry could be affected by changes in the regulation of GHG emissions. The Obama administration issued a number of rules that became effective in 2016. The Trump administration did change many of the rules that the Obama administration enacted.

Besides, I believe that in the coming years, the Trump administration will most likely change and relax certain GHG NSPS’s requirements. With that, I am not in a position to forecast when these changes will be effective, and whether they will enhance the valuation of the stock. In this regard, the annual report included the following commentary.

During the Obama Administration, the EPA finalized three rules that regulate GHG emissions from certain sources in the oil and natural gas industry, including New Source Performance Standards for the Oil and Natural Gas Sector, which became effective on August 2, 2016. During the Trump Administration, rulemaking was undertaken resulting in a substantial relaxation in the GHG NSPS’s requirements, including those relating to fugitive emissions, pneumatic pump standards, and closed vent system certification, among other things, which were finalized on August 13, 2020. Source: 10-k

There are also a number of new regulations issued by the SEC, which include a number of climate-related disclosures. NGL may have to invest a lot of money to understand and follow the new guidelines issued by the SEC. In addition, investors may not appreciate the information disclosed by NGL. As a result, the demand for the stock would most likely lower, which may lead to declines in the stock price. In this regard, NGL provided the following information in the last 10-k.

On March 6, 2024, the Securities and Exchange Commission (“SEC”) adopted a new set of rules that require a wide range of climate-related disclosures, including material climate-related risks, information on any climate-related targets or goals that are material to the registrant’s business, results of operations, or financial condition, Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions on a phased-in basis by certain larger registrants when those emissions are material and the filing of an attestation report covering the same, and disclosure of the financial statement effects of severe weather events and other natural conditions including costs and losses. Source: 10-k

Risks From Concentration Of Clients

The company reports that the number of clients does not seem that significant. 73% of the total amount of revenue from the Water Solutions segment came from ten clients. In the future, if the company loses one of these clients, the decline in total revenue could be significant. The crude oil logistics segment also seems to report a limited number of clients. Under the worst-case scenario, we could see a decline in total free cash flow, which may also lead to a significant decline in the stock price.

During the year ended March 31, 2025, 73% of the revenues of our Water Solutions segment were generated from our ten largest customers of the segment. Additionally, certain key customers of the Water Solutions segment contribute significantly to the cash flows and profitability of the Partnership. Any loss of those customers or their contracts could have an adverse impact on our financial results. Source: 10-k

During the year ended March 31, 2025, 79% of the revenues of our Crude Oil Logistics segment were generated from our ten largest customers of the segment. Source: 10-k

New Valuation Model

I increased my free cash flow expectations because of the recent increase in the oil price and the recent guidance delivered by NGL. My FCF figures include $477 million in 2025, $512 million in 2030, and $625 million in 2035. Let's note that my new numbers did not change a lot as compared to what I reported in my previous article.

I also used a conservative terminal multiple of 7x, WACC of 10%, and a share count of 132 million. The implied valuation stood at $8.5, which is higher than my target price in my previous report. In my previous article, my target price was close to $6.12 per share.

I do believe that there is upside potential in the stock price. However, considering the recent increase in the stock price, I no longer see such a large margin of safety to buy shares.

According to Seeking Alpha, the previous article provided stock returns of about 36%. I decided to close my position in the stock because I did identify other opportunities elsewhere.

I understand that investors out there maintain their position in the company because it is clearly undervalued. The company trades at 2x TTM cash flow. Peers trade at 5.58x TTM cash flow.

My Final Words

NGL Energy Partners recently provided a guidance that implies a valuation not far from 7.3x 2026 Adjusted EBITDA. Management also promised to undertake strategic actions to deliver stable free cash flow, as well as to simplify the company's business mix. In this regard, I believe that we can expect further sale of assets and net debt decreases. Given the recent increases in the price of oil, I redesigned my financial model, and increased my FCF forecasts, which implied a higher target price than that in the previous article. Having said that, and considering the recent increase in the stock price, I think that the margin of safety decreased. There is upside potential in the stock price, and the company is undervalued. However, I decided to close my position because I found other opportunities elsewhere. I understand that investors are not closing their position because the company continues to be cheap at 2x TTM cash flow.

data.title: VEA: A High-Conviction Case For Global Diversification Beyond U.S. Borders
data.description:
Intro

Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VEA) is one of the most widely held ETFs for developed market equity exposure outside the United States. With an ultra-low expense ratio of just 0.03% and over $224 billion in assets under management, it checks the boxes for cost-conscious, long-term investors. Yet despite its widespread use, VEA often finds itself on the defensive. Critics cite its historical underperformance relative to U.S. stocks, pointing to the S&P 500’s dominance over the past 10 to 15 years as evidence that international diversification “doesn’t work.”

But that framing is overly narrow, and frankly, misses the point of owning something like VEA.

Investing isn’t about loading up on yesterday’s winners. It’s about building durable, well-balanced portfolios that can survive different market environments and leadership rotations. VEA, by design, offers something U.S. stocks can’t: exposure to regions with different currencies, monetary policy regimes, sector weightings, and valuation profiles. And while the recent rearview mirror may show lagging returns, there are decades-long periods, including the early 2000s, when developed international stocks outperformed handily.

What’s more, VEA introduces sector diversification that is almost impossible to replicate through domestic U.S. holdings alone. It leans heavier into financials and industrials, with less exposure to U.S.-centric mega-cap tech names. This sector tilt adds behavioral diversification as well as different return paths, different reactions to interest rate changes, different exposure to global GDP.

Importantly, VEA isn’t just a placeholder for “non-U.S. stuff.” It’s a comprehensive, broad-market ETF with nearly 4,000 holdings. It avoids overlap with U.S. stocks entirely, making it ideal for portfolios that want clean, uncorrelated sleeves. And compared to its peers, funds like (IEFA), (ESGD), or (DBEF), it remains the lowest cost, highest scale, and arguably most trustworthy option, backed by Vanguard’s deep indexing experience.

In this article, we’ll unpack VEA’s portfolio role by examining key performance data, cyclical return behavior, sector diversification, top holdings, and how it stacks up against its international ETF competitors. We’ll also explore how tilting a portion of your developed markets exposure to funds like AVDV, the Avantis International Small Cap Value ETF, can amplify the benefits. Look to some of my articles pertaining for more details on this, though.

The argument here is not that VEA is poised to beat U.S. equities in the next year. It’s that over a full investing lifecycle, VEA plays a structurally different role, and that difference is what improves outcomes. If you believe that future leadership rotates, that rebalancing works, and that diversification means more than just owning more tickers, VEA is an asset that deserves a long-term seat at the table.

Performance Metrics Deep Dive

Let’s start with the raw numbers. From August 1990 to June 2025, Developed Markets (DM) produced a 5.57% CAGR. Meanwhile, U.S. Large Cap (US LC) delivered 10.51% CAGR. That’s a staggering difference over 35 years. But now look at the volatility numbers—DM carried higher volatility at 16.21% versus 14.79% for the U.S. Worse Sharpe. Worse Sortino. Bigger drawdowns. It's a tough look.

But zoom out: the 50/50 blend of DM and US LC returns 8.12% CAGR with a volatility profile similar to U.S. stocks. It even improves on downside risk, slightly lowering drawdown volatility. The Sharpe and Sortino ratios rise to 0.37 and 0.33, respectively, clearly better than DM alone. So while DM has been weaker, it still plays a useful role in combination. This is portfolio math 101: combining imperfectly correlated assets smooths outcomes. Blended portfolios aren't about picking winners, they're about creating structures that maximize stability and increase compoundability.

Long-Term Growth Path

The portfolio value chart visualizes the compounding in action. U.S. stocks pulled far ahead, no question. But the 50/50 DM/US LC portfolio compounded to a middle-ground path well above DM alone. That gap widens most notably post-2010 when U.S. tech dominance kicked in. But during earlier periods, such as the 1990s and early 2000s, Developed Markets kept pace and even outperformed for stretches.

Blending DM and U.S. equities helps to capture this broader opportunity set. Periodic rebalancing between the two allows investors to systematically buy low and sell high as leadership rotates. These are not theoretical gains, they’re real contributors to long-term wealth that only appear when you zoom out to multi-decade holding periods.

Annual Returns

The annual returns bar chart further illustrates how regional leadership shifts year-to-year. In years like 1993, 1994, 2004, 2005, and especially 2009, Developed Markets beat U.S. equities. These differences don’t just make for talking points, they create opportunities. Systematic rebalancing from relative outperformers to laggards takes advantage of mean reversion over time, enhancing returns at the portfolio level.

This return rotation is also crucial from a risk management standpoint. By holding VEA, you introduce non-U.S. exposure that responds differently to macroeconomic trends, interest rate cycles, and geopolitical dynamics. It’s not that VEA will outperform every year. In fact, it probably won’t. But its mere difference in behavior supports better diversification outcomes.

Five-Year CAGR Windows

One of the best ways to visualize cyclical leadership is to observe rolling five-year return windows. While U.S. equities tend to dominate the average, there are clear periods when Developed Markets shine. Between 2003 and 2008, and again around 2017, the red line (DM) rises above the blue line (US LC), showing periods of sustained outperformance. This is not noise, it’s rotation.

What makes these cycles powerful is their persistence. When DM outperforms, it often does so for multiple years, not just isolated events. Owning VEA gives you a claim on those stretches. More importantly, the 50/50 blend’s green line smooths the experience, reducing the chance of behavioral missteps from investors chasing performance at exactly the wrong time.

Distribution of Returns

Analyzing the return distribution adds another layer of insight. U.S. Large Cap returns are tightly clustered around moderate gains but with longer positive tails. DM returns are more dispersed, with more modest outcomes and occasional sharp drawdowns. When these two are combined into a blended portfolio, the distribution becomes taller, narrower, and more stable.

This matters for both retirees and accumulators. For retirees drawing from their portfolio, fewer extreme months reduce the likelihood of sequence risk—that is, poor returns early in retirement that jeopardize sustainability. For younger investors, smoother monthly performance builds confidence, increasing the likelihood they’ll stay invested through volatility.

Drawdowns and Recovery

The drawdown chart reveals that DM investors endured a 57.37% peak-to-trough decline, which took over 5 years to fully recover. U.S. equities experienced a slightly shallower 50.97% drawdown but bounced back in just under 3.5 years. The 50/50 blend split the difference, a slightly smaller drawdown and faster recovery.

This might sound marginal, but during real-time stress events, even small improvements in drawdown duration and depth can make the difference between staying the course and panic-selling. VEA alone is hard to hold during these events. But as part of a diversified blend, it reduces overall fragility.

Correlations and Complementarity

Correlation between U.S. and Developed Markets equity is 0.84. This is not independent, but low enough to offer diversification. Compare that to U.S. Large and Mid Cap stocks, which often exhibit correlations over 0.95. When constructing portfolios, this marginal difference enables more effective rebalancing and dampens volatility clusters.

What makes VEA unique is not just that it's international, it’s that it offers exposure to different policy regimes, economic sensitivities, and sectoral weightings. In short, it introduces return paths you can't get from U.S. equities alone. That’s real diversification, not just holding more stocks, but holding different drivers of risk and return.

Peer Comparison

One of VEA’s most compelling advantages is its simplicity and cost structure. At just 0.03% in expenses, it is among the lowest-cost international ETFs available. This makes it ideal for long-term investors seeking efficiency. In fact, when stacked up against other international equity funds like (IEFA) (0.07%), (ESGD) (0.21%), (DBEF) (0.35%), (GSIE) (0.25%), and (RODM) (0.29%), VEA clearly leads on pricing.

But the edge doesn’t stop at cost. VEA also leads in scale. With over $224 billion in assets under management (AUM), it is by far the largest developed market ETF, eclipsing the next largest, IEFA, at $138 billion. That scale matters. Higher AUM often correlates with tighter bid-ask spreads, better tax efficiency, and stronger liquidity—especially during periods of stress or rebalancing. For institutional or high-volume traders, that reliability is a hidden but vital feature.

Another underrated aspect of VEA is its brand. Vanguard has a long-standing reputation for investor-first practices, low costs, and rigorous index construction. In an environment where fund families continue to launch thematic or narrow-focus products with unclear mandates, VEA’s broad, consistent, and rules-based approach is both refreshing and valuable. Vanguard’s stewardship, scale, and trust give long-term allocators the confidence to stick with it through periods of underperformance.

Diversification and Structure

Beyond price and reputation, VEA is structurally sound in ways that complement U.S.-centric portfolios. The sector breakdown adds meaningful diversification. Unlike the S&P 500, which is heavily tilted toward technology and communications, VEA’s largest sector weightings are in Financials (22.91%) and Industrials (18.34%), followed by Technology (10.27%), Health Care (10.04%), and Consumer Cyclical (9.36%).

This makes VEA less reliant on mega-cap tech and more exposed to economic cyclicals, global banks, industrial machinery, and pharmaceuticals. These are sectors that tend to do well during periods of global economic growth or reflation, providing valuable rotation potential when U.S. growth stocks stumble. That’s not just sector diversification; it’s behavioral diversification too. Different sectors react to different shocks. Holding them together smooths the overall ride.

The diversification is also geographic and monetary. With no U.S. exposure, VEA avoids duplication. Its 3,800+ holdings span Europe, Japan, Australia, and Canada, exposing investors to varied fiscal and monetary regimes, demographics, and currencies. This makes VEA one of the most complete international funds on the market.

Top Holdings Insight

VEA’s top 10 holdings as of April 2025 are led by SAP SE (SAP), Nestle SA ( OTCPK:NSRGY) , ASML (ASML) , Novartis (NVS) , and Toyota (OTCPK:TOYOF). These firms operate in industries such as enterprise software, semiconductors, consumer staples, and pharmaceuticals. Importantly, these companies represent real economic activity in Europe and Asia, uncorrelated with U.S. tech earnings cycles.

Additionally, none of the top 10 exceed a 1.32% portfolio weight. This makes the fund relatively unconcentrated at the top. Compare that to U.S. ETFs, where Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) can often account for 7–10% combined. This structural dispersion reduces single-stock risk and adds to portfolio resilience.

In total, VEA holds 3,886 stocks, offering extremely broad exposure without overconcentration in any one firm or sector. For investors who want to avoid top-heavy portfolios while still capturing international growth, VEA’s structure is hard to beat.

Conclusion

VEA isn’t a home run hitter. It’s not the fastest horse in the race, and it likely won’t top any return leaderboard over the next 12 months. But that doesn’t mean it doesn’t matter. In fact, the more seriously you take portfolio construction, the more you realize that assets like VEA are exactly what keep portfolios intact, not because they dominate, but because they do something even more valuable: they diversify what matters.

Developed Markets have underperformed U.S. equities for much of the past decade, but that underperformance has not been linear, and it won’t last forever. Asset class leadership rotates. Valuations mean-revert. Global economic cycles change. And when those shifts come, the benefits of holding a truly global equity sleeve are amplified, not just through performance, but through improved rebalancing opportunities, smoother multi-asset dynamics, and reduced sequence risk.

VEA is also unmatched in structural efficiency. It offers clean, complete exposure to non-U.S. developed economies at a fraction of the cost of its peers. It avoids overlap with U.S. stocks. It’s not concentrated in a handful of mega-cap names. It owns nearly 4,000 stocks across dozens of countries and sectors, and does it all for three basis points. You can’t build that on your own.

Beyond just data, there’s also a behavioral layer to this argument. A portfolio that contains assets that behave differently, especially ones like VEA that zig when others zag, is a portfolio that helps investors stick to the plan. And staying invested matters more than squeezing out marginal performance. VEA reduces fragility, increases discipline, and gives investors the emotional bandwidth to withstand future uncertainty.

For those seeking to go further, tilting VEA’s exposure with a value-focused ETF like AVDV adds additional upside potential. AVDV complements VEA’s broad market beta with proven factor exposure to international small-cap value stocks, an equity sleeve that has historically delivered stronger risk-adjusted returns while remaining uncorrelated to U.S. large-cap growth.

In total, VEA may never be the most exciting fund in your portfolio. But over 30 years, it may be one of the most important. Not because it outperforms every year, but because it plays a role no other asset class can replicate: a differentiated, diversified, low-cost counterbalance to the U.S. equity machine. And when the cycle turns, as it always does, those who made space for VEA will be glad they did.

Attribution

All charts and performance data sourced from factorlytics.io as of June 2025. Holdings, sector data, and peer ETF comparisons retrieved from Vanguard and Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Dino Polska: Mixed Q1 Results, But Long Term Growth Is On Track
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Dino Polska (OTCPK:DNOPY) was on March 16. In that article, I gave the company a buy rating. Since then, it has returned 14.42% compared to the S&P 500's 6.13%. Despite the positive performance difference, Dino is down nearly 10% from its all-time highs. Additionally, it released its Q1 results in the meantime, which were apparently weaker than expected — but there's an explanation.

I always say that Dino is somewhat opaque when it comes to their communications. They don’t hold earnings conference calls, so I’ll rely on the presentation and the report they shared to write this article.

Thesis Review

This is a fragment from my previous article that I believe summarizes the company very well.

Let's briefly recall the thesis for Dino Polska. Dino is a Polish supermarket chain with a strong presence in the western part of the country. It's characterized by having a founder-led management, high vertical integration, ownership of its store locations and the land it operates on, as well as its own meat processing plants and distribution centers.

The company's main competitive advantage lies in its "local monopolies" in rural areas of the country. By focusing mainly on towns with no more than 5,000 inhabitants and maintaining a high level of agility in building stores (the company owns the firm that constructs its supermarkets), Dino makes it unprofitable for other competitors to enter these market niches.

If we recall, Dino's long-term bullish thesis is based on its ability to continue expanding the number of stores in Poland (currently 2,688) while maintaining organic growth above inflation. We will see how well this combination holds up, but it should drive sales growth in the mid-single-digit range. With good capital allocation, bottom-line growth should be in the high-double-digit range. Let's review its figures.

Earnings Review

Here we have several highlights from the quarter presented by the company. Let’s take a closer look at a few of them.

The first thing I would highlight, one of the pillars of the investment thesis, is that the pace of new store openings has reaccelerated (58 vs. 32 in Q1 2024). This is good news, especially considering Dino’s still significant total addressable market, which is estimated at at least 7,000 stores within Poland. This is undoubtedly one of the company’s key growth drivers and the main reason behind its capital reinvestment (more on this later).

My estimates for this year are that Dino will end with around 3,000 owned stores, which would represent a year-over-year growth rate of about 12%, or roughly 320 new stores (50 more than last year). If they maintain this accelerated pace of openings, it seems plausible.

Other highlights related to real estate and environmental measures. This continued progress is positive, as it makes Dino’s stores energy self-sufficient, which will ultimately improve margins through cost savings.

In the first quarter of 2025, a total of 2,569 Dino stores were equipped with their own photovoltaic installations, representing an increase of 93 stores compared to the previous quarter. The total capacity of the solar panels installed in these stores reached 103 MW, marking a 17% year-over-year growth. During this period, Dino stores generated 15.2 GWh of electricity from solar energy, compared to 10.4 GWh generated in the same quarter of 2024.

Without a doubt, the most concerning aspect in light of inflation was the company’s like-for-like (LFL) growth of just 0.5% compared to the same quarter last year. It’s very unusual for Dino to grow below the inflation rate (which was 6.1% in this case), but there is context behind it.

According to the Q1 report:

LFL sales growth in Q1 2025 was 0.5%, following 11.9% growth in the corresponding period of 2024. The drivers contributing to the relatively low LFL sales growth rate in Q1 2025 were as follows: i) the shift in the timing of the Easter Holiday from Q1 2024 to Q2 2025 (which in turn should favorably affect the LFL sales growth rate in Q2 of this year) and ii) two fewer trading days in Q1 2025 versus Q1 2024.

In the first quarter of 2025, costs as a percentage of sales remained relatively stable or showed slight improvements. The cost of sales accounted for 75.7% of revenue, slightly below the 76.9% recorded in Q1 2024, indicating a modest improvement in gross margin. However, selling and marketing expenses increased as a proportion of revenue, driven by the expansion of the store network and comparable sales growth. General and administrative expenses also rose, though to a lesser extent. Overall, while absolute costs increased, the cost of sales as a percentage of revenue showed a slight efficiency gain.

While the EBITDA margin has slightly declined, it’s due to higher marketing expenses tied to the accelerated pace of new store openings — which makes sense. The clear positive, however, is the increase in gross margin, a sign that the economy remains focused on cost savings and that Dino’s scale allows it to negotiate better prices with suppliers. This represents an operational improvement, whereas the rise in OPEX is a self-inflicted effect of the current expansion phase. I remain confident.

It’s clearly visible in the image.

As expected, the accelerated pace of store openings has significantly increased CAPEX (from 330M to 516M). Far from being a cause for concern, it’s something I’m actually pleased to see. This is what I wrote in my previous article:

If we look at its cash generation, we can see that Dino is in positive FCF territory for the second consecutive year.

After reinvesting in the business through CAPEX (1.6B PLN), the 951M PLN generated in FCF has primarily been used to repay debt. My hypothesis is that in the coming years, Dino will start distributing dividends or repurchasing shares.

Until 2022, FCF was negative because Dino reinvested everything into opening new stores. We have now reached a point where the company generates so much cash that it will have to start returning it to investors.

But considering that Dino has ROICs of around 23%, it’s preferable that the company continues reinvesting in the business (as long as management expects similar returns) rather than distributing capital to shareholders. Given the TAM still ahead, I’d rather see them keep opening stores and gaining scale. The time to become a cash cow and start paying dividends and doing buybacks will come later.

On the other hand, debt continues to decline and is now down to just 0.31x.

Valuation

On the valuation side, nothing has changed—except that with the recent increase in the share price, the expected returns have gone from 12.3% back in March to around 11% currently. Just as a reminder, I’m projecting an annual store opening rate of 10–12%, 5% LFL growth, and an EBITDA margin of 8.5% by 2029. If we assign a 15x multiple, the resulting market cap would be 84,000 million PLN versus the current 50,000 million.

For all these reasons, I’m maintaining my buy rating, despite the stock price losing some momentum in the short term.

Risks

The risks the company faces remain unchanged.

Taking into account that it operates in a market niche with little technological disruption, offering a significant value proposition for the customer, boasting strong competitive advantages, and being capable of passing cost inflation increases onto higher prices, perhaps the company's greatest risk lies in key personnel.

As we have already discussed, Dino's CEO is its founder and has been responsible for evolving the company to its current state. While he is still 50 years old and heavily involved in the company's day-to-day operations, a change in leadership could entail a shift in business strategy and its consequent performance. Although nothing has been mentioned regarding this matter and he still has many years until retirement, I consider this risk to be the greatest for Dino."

However, the second biggest risk facing this type of sector is competition. It's important to keep in mind that supermarkets don't create switching costs or extreme brand loyalty. This is why it tends to be a sector with very fierce competition. While Dino tends to outperform its rivals, the differences often end up being determined by management. We'll have to monitor industry trends to see if Dino is being overtaken by the competition.

Conclusion

And that’s all the information from the Q1 results the company has presented. One might consider these results somewhat mixed, but the drop in sales and margin is understandable and has clear reasons behind it. On the other hand, the increase in reinvestment is a positive development, and everything seems to indicate that Dino is on track to meet my store opening estimates.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amcor: Berry Global Acquisition To More Than Double Cash Flows, Stock Near All-Time Lows
data.description:
Amcor (NYSE:AMCR)(OTCPK:AMCCF) has been struggling with the environment after the pandemic boom, but is now potentially getting a second chance to shine following the Berry Global acquisition. With the stock now trading even below the pre-pandemic levels, a solid dividend and a lot of synergies expected, I rate Amcor a Buy now, with an attractive risk-reward overall. As an essential company in many global FMCG (fast-moving consumer goods) industries paying very strong dividends, this is now getting to the level I've been waiting for to consider buying.

Introduction & Financials

Amcor is a leader in consumer and healthcare packaging with a broad global footprint, providing cartons, closures, flexible films and other types of packaging for several global brands such as Coca-Cola (KO), Colgate‑Palmolive (CL), PepsiCo (PEP), Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), Mars, L'Oréal (OTCPK:LRLCF), McDonald’s (MCD) and many others. They provide packaging solutions for a very wide range of industries, from food, beverages and pet food to home care and personal care, being one of the backbones of our modern society and key for many brands around us.

They can make around $800 million in cash flow now and expect $900 million to $1 billion in adjusted free cash flow in this fiscal year, normalizing around a solid level after a significant boom in demand. The company saw a rise in household consumption (and thus demand for packaging) during the pandemic thanks to stay-at-home trends, but that is now falling due to what they call increasingly uncertain consumer environment, particularly in the US, although it’s still significantly above pre-pandemic levels thanks to an acquisition I’ll mention below.

On the margin side, Amcor is below the industry’s average EBITDA margin of ~15.9% last year, according to research from Capstone Partners. Still, given their size as the industry leader, this is not terrible on such a revenue, and we actually see improvements in the past 12 months, although it’s still slightly below their 5Y average of 14.36% due to ongoing issues in the environment.

Financially, since Amcor recently bought Berry Global, one of their key competitors, we’ll have to look into both of their balance sheets since they have yet to release a combined one. On Amcor’s side, we see an overall good position, with current assets above their current liabilities, but also a significant amount of debt after recently issuing $2.2 billion to finance the merger with Berry that I’ll mention below. Their debt comes at relatively low interest rates of 1.125 - 5.625%, with all of them being spread well over the next 8 years and no significant maturity in any. So, not a bad balance sheet overall, although there isn’t much equity, especially if we deduct the goodwill.

From Berry, we see a similar position, with relatively high debt offset by their assets, although also a significant amount of goodwill. Still, they should be okay from a financial point of view, as no debt came with significant maturities that they couldn’t refinance. If anything, Amcor’s size could help them refinance this at better than Berry’s recent weighted average interest of ~ 5.37%, so that’s maybe another $50-100 million in interest saved.

Risks & Opportunities

A reason behind the post-pandemic wave of growth, on top of the higher demand, is the acquisition of Bemis in 2019 for around $5.25 billion, combined with structural cost cuts and investments in higher-growth areas. However, we see this repeating itself at an even greater scale now, in what seems to be one of their best moves to date, with Amcor recently announcing another significant acquisition - purchasing Berry Global for ~$8.4 billion worth of stock. Following the acquisition, they expect 12% EPS accretion in FY26 through synergy benefits alone 35%+ EPS accretion by end of FY28 through $650 million total synergies (not annual).

The acquisition is expected to significantly increase its debt load (as I mentioned above) but should also generate very significant cash flows. With Berry itself generating more than $600-800 million in cash flows in previous years, this should be very beneficial for Amcor. This would basically more than double Amcor’s TTM free cash flow. By FY28, they expect annual operating cash flows of over $3 billion, so a very significant level of growth. It’s no wonder that over 99% of Amcor shares present voted in favor of the deal.

Another potentially positive catalyst are rate cuts boosting the economy, but due to Trump’s tariffs, we now saw the Fed deciding not to cut in June due to expecting elevated inflation and weaker economic growth. Still, 2 rate cuts are expected this year, so it’s a step forward to this catalyst. I consider this a catalyst because rates are one of the tools that central banks have in fighting inflation. By increasing the rates, the Fed increases the cost of borrowing, which limits discretionary budgets, thus lowering spending and demand and fighting inflation. When they are cut, the opposite happens, basically boosting consumer demand and discretionary budgets, which is beneficial for Amcor and their clients.

Another fundamental tailwind to keep in mind is the growing population. Amcor’s products are key for many FMCG globally, and a higher population attracts by default higher consumption of food/beverages/personal care/etc. products, which means sustained demand for companies like Amcor and Berry.

Dividends & Buybacks

Amcor is a Dividend Aristocrat with over 40 years of consecutive dividend hikes, currently trading at around their highest yields ever - ~5.6% - representing around $730 million, which is on the brink of not being sustainable. However, thanks to the Berry Global acquisition, this should save the company for several more years. This acquisition literally saved Amcor from potentially cutting the dividend soon and seeing its stock price crash. As a result of the Berry merger, there will be ~2.37 billion shares of the new Amcor based on their SEC filing, so the total dividend should cost them around $1.1 billion from now on, plus other future increases.

They also did significant buybacks back in 2022 and 2023, but reduced them a lot after the environment worsened in recent years. This extra demand also contributed to the ups and downs we saw in the stock price during that time, and there is potential to see them spend the extra capital on that again after the Berry deal, although I’d prefer seeing them focus on repaying the debt and improving the balance sheet first. From the looks of it, they expect the yield to fall to 3-4% from the historic 4-5% as they’ll ramp up the buybacks after the acquisition is complete.

Valuation

Based on a DCF valuation where Amcor’s cost of capital falls to 6% thanks to rate cuts, and using a starting free cash flow of $1.4 billion (roughly equal to their combined TTM), growing at 5% annually for five years and 3% for the following five (well below Amcor’s 5Y average of 6.3%), with a 1.5% terminal growth rate, we get to an enterprise value of ~$39.0 billion. After subtracting roughly $17.6 billion in net debt following the Berry acquisition, the implied equity value is ~$21.4 billion. With ~2.37 billion shares outstanding now, this results in an intrinsic value of roughly $9.04 per share, which is literally 1 cent below the price this is trading at when writing this. However, let’s also see some sensitivity scenarios to get a better idea about the merger’s potential.

Scenario 1

In a bad environment with basically no synergies and a slow CAGR of 2.5%, the combined companies should make around $1.5 billion in 3 years. If the market is disappointed by this, we can even assume a worse ratio than we’ve seen in recent years - let’s say 12, which would be ~20% below the packaging industry’s median of ~15. That’s a market cap of $18 billion, meaning a price of $7.88, so about 20% below the current stock price.

In this case, their ~$1.1 billion dividend would still be sustainable, and there is enough room to improve the business by repaying debt on top. In this scenario, the yield would be around 6.1%, so that by itself, combined with very small hikes, could be enough to offset the loss. Still, that’s not a good enough return for 3 years, but this scenario assumes significant overstatements about the merger from the company.

Scenario 2

If they reach their $3 billion OpCF target, and we subtract roughly $1 billion in CAPEX (their combined levels from recent years), that’s $2 billion in FCF by 2028, implying a solid CAGR of ~10% for 3 years, which is not impossible to see thanks to their synergies and potential debt repayments/lower interest rates. If the market gets more optimistic about it and trades at 22.5x that amount - similar to 2023 levels - that’s a $45 billion market cap, meaning a price of $19.70, so a very nice ~110% profit from the current level, plus significant dividends that they can even hike to 7-8% sustainably.

Conclusions

So, based on the current levels, Amcor seems like it’s fairly valued for a low-rate environment and low growth expectations. However, for a solid 5.6% dividend yield and upside potential from the acquisition in case they beat the market’s current expectations, I believe Amcor could be a Buy at the current levels - although I’d prefer to see a focus on debt repayments for a while.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Strawberry Fields: Bought At $9.8/Share, Upside Potential Was Here
data.description:
I've mentioned in a few of my past articles that I recently in 2024-2025 left behind much of my US-based allocation due to correctly proven assumptions of FX overvaluation, thus avoiding a downturn. As things stand now, with FX for USD/SEK and USD/EUR reduced, NA is becoming more attractive for investing again. However, there are companies that were exceptions to my rule of not investing in the US at the time, and one of those exceptions, albeit a small one, is one I'm going to look at today. I will show you why Strawberry Fields REIT (NYSE:STRW) is a company you'll want to keep an eye on - much like I've kept an eye on it in 2025.

As I mentioned in the article found here, I actually have a position in the REIT, but as of my latest update, I considered it a "HOLD" with a sub-$10/share PT. That assumption/rating has outperformed since that time, avoiding a decline of about 3-5%, with the S&P500 outperforming by about 12%.

And while I am going to be raising some excellent, positive points for the company here, I want to remind you what Strawberry Fields, in the end, is. It's a small REIT in a narrow industry and while well-managed, there are much bigger companies with safer fundamentals out there. This is why my position in the company, despite the positivity I'm about to share, is small.

Let's see what we have for STRW as of 1H25, and going into the next few years.

Strawberry Fields REIT - long-term potential upside is here, and outperformance is possible.

My last article on the company is roughly 3 months old at this time. My reason for updating at this time is really only one - I recently bought more Strawberry Fields, because the company for a short time, met my investment criteria and my PT of below $10/share. For a short time a few weeks back in May of 2025, it traded at below $10/share, setting off an alert at my broker.

Now, because, the USD/SEK FX pairing at the time was below 9.5 SEK per USD compared to over 11 SEK at it's high, I managed to scoop up shares at $9.8 per share.

Why did I do this?

First off, STRW at that price yielded over 5.1% - which for this REIT is excellent. Even with a market capitalization of less than $150M, this is a company I like. The uplisting from OTC to NYSE was a big reason for me to increase me interest back in -24 (uplisting in -23), but rather, this is a company that's been grown from the "ground up" by current management, and done so extremely skillfully, judging by the way that the CEO has managed to generate AFFO and growth over a period of 2 decades.

As I mentioned in my previous article, the company's CEO started buying up skilled nursing facilities, which now number in excess of 130 in 11 states around the US. Strawberry Fields has a very strong experience of this type of triple-net leasing, and unlike other REITs in the field, tenant concentration is not that big a deal.

The company generates AFFO in excess of $16M per quarter, confirmed in the recently-reported 1Q25 period, with almost $17M of AFFO, or over $60M of AFFO per year. In terms of leverage or debt, which is the keystone to any REIT's success, the company's debt is all fixed, low-interest, and long-term HUD guaranteed. The average interest rate for the company is 3.91%, which is comparable to European companies in the same sector, but European companies have about 40-150x the market cap of this company. The company's low interest rate is part of what In my view, makes STRW so interesting. Furthermore, this debt has a maturity of over 20 years.

You may not get 100-200% RoR in the near term - I don't see STRW managing this - but you will be flying under the radar with a very appealing sort of exposure, while earning about 5% yield on your invested capital in. dividends alone.

So, leverage and debt remain a "check" for 1Q25. Nothing has changed in terms of risk. The company's track record of growth has also remained steady, with adjusted EBITDA and FFO growth in the double digits, for FFO, almost 14% CAGR.

Strawberry Fields, and its management under CEO Mr. Moishe Gubin, has shown itself to be extremely risk-averse. The company's slow growth over time, it's 20+ year history prior to listing, its sub-52% net leverage ratio, and also its 100% rent collections over time confirm this to me.

The company's tenancy has a mostly geographical profile - meaning that tenants are usually concentrated in geographical areas, which I actually like. Why do I like this?

Because it implies that the company's tenants have a wealth of local experience and efficiency.

The company, as you can see, even states that it's a focus for STRW to acquire geographically concentrated facilities. I believe that the failures or risks of skilled nursing/healthcare operators are well-known to most of the companies in the sector, also emerging ones - and when it comes to STRW, the company seems very insistent not to make similar mistakes (over-leverage, tenant risks) as others.

The company's M&A strategy and underwriting criteria is extremely conservative, even when compared to other companies in the field - but STRW also continues to find assets for purchase, several in 2025 in fact.

The company's focus on off-market smaller deals means that the competition for the types of deals that the company does is very limited - sometimes it's likely that STRW is the only, or one of the only interested parties involved.

While a lot of published negativity has concerned the nursing home industry across the US, with even me having done some poor investments in the sector, the fact is that the moats to this industry are not insignificant. A combination of significant construction costs (due to regulation and knowledge requirement), with a complex funding system with federal and state-level subsidies through the combination of Medicaid and Medicare, has resulted in a very low supply of nursing homes and licensing restrictions. From a personal standpoint, I have experience in the industry as well, though not in the US, as I, on a director level, managed five skilled nursing homes with a total of 165 beds in my time as an external consulting director for a public institution during 2022-2024.

The moats I speak of are not unique to the US - these requirements and hesitancy for new supply are present in Europe/Sweden as well, due to the requirements when constructing these buildings. Even public organizations are very hesitant to build new ones - and this creates a moat for any company that operates them expertly. Despite the differences in our systems, I recognize the value of such an operator. That is in fact why I became interested in the first place - I saw the things the company was doing, and recognized stability that we really didn't see in other, bigger companies.

Strawberry Fields is therefore on a growth trajectory. I believe this growth trajectory to be intact even for the future. As long as the company applies its conservative underwriting and investment approach, I see no reason why the company should do significantly worse or differently than it has to this time. The company is in a position where it's "flying below the radar", so it can comfortably acquire off-market deals using the same logic for years to come, rewarding shareholders with both dividends and stability. At least theoretically.

One of the company's benefits is also debt issuances, which, unlike other US-based companies, are done on the Tel Aviv stock exchange, due to the management background and ties to Israel. I view this as an incredible advantage due to the long maturity times.

On the flip side, as I mentioned, one of the most important things is not to expect outsized growth. It's my firm stance that Strawberry Fields should trade in a very narrow accepted valuation corridor. Any move to the downside of that corridor that is not predicated by worsening of fundamentals is a signal to potentially "BUY" - while any move to the upside, unless the company is seeing significant advantages, is a move to do the opposite - to rotate.

So I keep a close eye on STRW, and as of 1Q25, my valuation thesis is as follows.

Valuation for Strawberry Fields

My case for Strawberry Fields hasn't changed much since my last article. At the time, I gave the company a conservative PT of $10/share based on a fair value of around $13.5-$14.5/share. That's also, as it happens, the corridor of which I spoke.

I expect and forecast an AFFO growth rate of about 9-10% per year, slightly below the company's previous average, to apply a slight discount to growth, and account for the somewhat riskier macro overall. However, I believe that STRW should trade at least at a 9.5x P/AFFO rate to account for the positive qualities and growth that we've seen. At a 9.5x P/AFFO, the company currently has a forward growth/return potential of around 13.5% per year - lower than my hurdle. However, when I bought at $9.8/share, the company's upside to this same multiple was over 16% per year. I consider the company's 9-10x P/AFFO multiple also to be correct, because it represents nearly a 50% multiple discount to the larger players in this industry, reflecting its size risk.

The company's drops to a valuation where the upside is appealing are, as you can see in the trend above, usually very short. The company usually bounces up fairly quickly, as it has with my recent buy, hence why I'm at a decent profit for my position. The best approach one could do for STRW, I believe, to put a price alert below $10/share - as I had the previous time around.

However, due to confirmed AFFO growth in 1Q25, and my expectation for this growth to continue, I am raising my PT to one of $10.5/share - and also raising my alert to that level. Given the company's small size, I'm allowing for quite a bit of valuation and pricing, but this has to do with the positive qualities I mentioned. I can also show you that the company has significantly outperformed the broader market, with much bigger peers, that are also much riskier in terms of operator risk.

Granted, you can't (in my view) say that STRW, a company with a sub-$200M market capitalization, should trade at the 15-16x P/AFFO multiple of say, NHI or Caretrust. That would make no sense, given the size differences. But I would say at a 9-10x P/AFFO valuation, the company's size is not an obstacle to investing in it - not under this management, and not with this operating history. The company has proven, over time, that it does not overuse debt to fund risky acquisitions, in fact, that it does not do risky acquisitions in a way that threatens its survival. It seems very comfortable with a "slow growth", and this is something that I both respect and appreciate in an investment like this.

It's something I can get behind - much like I got behind the investment approach for Agree Realty (ADC) when first investing in the company. I sold off ADC for a combination of FX and valuation reasons back in early 2025 with a very nice profit, but would be thrilled to get back in at a low, appealing multiple.

Strawberry Fields has some of the same appeal as this, $8.3B market cap REIT. and that should not be ignored.

I should also mention, to any Israel-based investor, the company has a native Tel Aviv ticker, STRW, which currently trades at ₪38 - so if you have access to this, there might be tax advantages that I am unaware of.

Risks to my thesis

Risks exist to every company. Because I rate the company as "HOLD", there is both an upside and downside risk. The upside risk is that I am too conservative, and the company could see a sudden increase in valuation. Aside from the size variable, I have no reason or background in saying the company shouldn't be above 10x P/AFFO. It's a straight peer-based assumption, with no real argument beyond this, aside from the fact that this is where the company has been for the past 2 years. I should also mention in terms of upside risk that my PT of $10.5/share is the lower end of the range. 7 Analysts follow STRW, and they have the company at an average of $13.1/share, with 6 out of 7 at a "BUY" recommendation here. I am therefore going against the grain - with my reason being that I believe a small company should be discounted.

On the downside, if the company were to underperform, such as sticking its 2-year current AFFO average of 8.4x, that would in turn spell a return of less than 8% per year, inclusive of dividends. So there's a scenario here where you are certainly not making as much money as in some safer stocks.

But it's a value play - and I don't see value destruction or fundamental danger as a real possibility. So, despite the company size, I view overall risk as low to moderate, and I would argue with anyone saying that this company is "speculative" simply because of its size.

Thesis

  • Strawberry REIT is an attractive skilled nursing REIT under superb management with a great upside at the right sort of valuation - unfortunately, we currently don't have the "right" valuation to make it work, though we had it a few weeks ago when I bought at $9.8/share.
  • I have a small in this somewhat thinly traded REIT, and one I intend to keep unless the company goes above $14/share. If that were to happen, I would then probably rotate my stake - just as I would continue to buy more at below my updated PT of $10.5/share.
  • After the reported 1Q25, I am upgrading my PT to $10.5/share, but due to a valuation higher than this, I cannot change my rating at this time, and continue to say "HOLD".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $10.5/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of June 2025

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. PCE, GDP, Canadian CPI And Many Speeches From Heads Of Central Banks
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: Central Bank Rate decisions and ongoing Israel-Iran conflict

Geopolitical tensions remain high as Iran continues launching ballistic missiles toward Israel, prompting retaliatory strikes from Israel on Iranian military and nuclear infrastructure.

While headlines are still arriving by the minute, the market's sensitivity has declined somewhat this week, with the conflict increasingly priced in.

Equity indices corrected earlier but rebounded midweek - now selling off again as traders close positions ahead of the weekend, pricing in renewed geopolitical risk.

What has stirred markets more recently is the possibility of U.S. intervention in the conflict. President Trump has indicated a decision may come within the next two weeks, adding a layer of uncertainty.

For position traders, it's worth noting the elevated risk of price gaps when markets reopen on Sunday evening.

U.S. crude oil is trading higher on the week but has pulled back after a failed breakout attempt near key resistance, closing the week around $75.

On the monetary policy front, major central banks - including the Fed, Bank of England, Swiss National Bank, Bank of Japan, and People's Bank of China - held rate decisions this week.

Only the SNB moved, cutting rates to 0% and signaling that a return to negative territory remains on the table if the Swiss franc strengthens excessively. This reflects growing concern over a slowing domestic economy and weakening exports.

In forex, the U.S. dollar staged a comeback as dollar selling positions became overcrowded, and risk sentiment tilted more cautious. Traders are leaning back into the greenback amid global uncertainty.

The Week ahead: Inflation reports, Central Bank heads scheduled

Keep your expectations for volatility high, with conflict headlines still being released throughout the day in every session and a potential US Intervention that would surely be market moving.

Asia Pacific Markets - Australian CPI and another Inflation data point for Japan

APAC Markets aren't the busiest in the upcoming week, but one thing to look at is a reversal of previous week's gains on currencies such as the AUD and NZD, which had decent performance since the RBA and RBNZ Rate Decisions.

Tuesday evening should see the release of the Australian May Inflation report, expected at 2.4% year-over-year.

Further reports on Japanese inflation for June are also expected on Thursday at 19:30 ET, with the Core data expected at 3.3% y/y.

Economic Data from Europe, UK and North America - US GDP, PCE and Canada CPI

This week also won't be the busiest in terms of data release; however, markets will surely brace for both the US GDP and the Core PCE as markets keep assembling data on the effect of US Tariffs on the world's largest economy.

Core PCE data is expected at 0.1% month-over-month (Friday morning 8:30 ET) and the annualized Q1 GDP figure expected at -0.2% on Thursday at 8:30 A.M.

Canada will also see the release of their own CPI report on Wednesday at 8:30, with the Headline data forecasted at 1.5% and Core CPI expected at 2.6%.

Other than data, many Heads of central banks will be speaking with ECB's Lagarde on Monday speaking at the European Parliament in Brussels, Fed Chair Powell testifying on Wednesday (usually accompanied by a speech on Monetary outlooks), BOE's Bailey talking at the British Chambers of Commerce Global Annual Conference on Thursday.

Fed's Williams will also be speaking on Friday (7:30) in Basel, Switzerland, where we will see his own view on the upcoming Rate Decision after this morning's dovish surprise from Chris Waller.

Also, as always, stay in touch with the latest developments in the Middle East to avoid being on the wrong side of sudden volatility spikes!

For all market-moving economic releases and events, see the MarketPulse Economic Calendar.

US Dollar performance versus other Forex Major counterparts

Have a good weekend, and safe trades for the week ahead!

Original Post

data.title: Humble Group's Growth Belongs To Future Snacking, But The Market Remains Skeptical
data.description:
Introduction

Humble group is a Swedish company that operates within the food industry. They develop and distribute different types of sugar and calorie reduced products. This article is about the ticker Humble Group (OTCPK:HMBAF), but the article is more aimed for the investors who can access the Nasdaq Stockholm where this stock has much higher volume, and goes under the ticker “HUMBLE”. I believe Humble Group holds a lot of potential, but is currently on a negative spiral due to bad performance last quarter. Humble's Group shares are falling, and I don't believe this is the right time to buy it; therefore, I rate this stock a HOLD!

What has happened?

Humble Group financial history has shown signs of strong growth in the past four years. In 2020, they had revenues just slightly over SEK 33 million, and in 2024 they sold products for over SEK 7,700 million, that is quite the growth in only four years. Humble Group has used leverage and IPO capital to acquire new companies that fit their business model and grow even faster, and many of these businesses have seen immense growth in the past years. However, Humble group did not become profitable until 2023, so it is a rather young company with already strong presence in the Scandinavian market.

Humble Group has met some difficulties going into 2025. The main ticker on Nasdaq Stockholm is down more than 30% this year. There could be several reasons why, but the main reason could be investors worried about market share and pause in growth. The CEO mentioned that part of the slowdown comes from Easter.

The late Easter implies that a large proportion of sell-in will take place in April, while we see good conditions to deliver higher organic growth during the remainder of the year.

Humble group has a high multiple valuation due to its strong growth in the past years, but it is seeing pressure right now on its valuation.

Humble Group AB sales were up 4% YoY last quarter, notably was gross profit up 9% which shows Humble Group was able to increase gross margin due to optimize purchasing and shipping more cost-efficient. Profitability was stable around 5% both this quarter and TTM, only if we exclude non items affecting comparability. Humble Group had cost worth of SEK 18 million related to restructuring costs, Lock-in penalty from acquisition SPA and other.

Future snacking was up 19% YoY and reached SEK 282 million in sales last quarter. Future snacking saw strong demand throughout their portfolio, with key demand for confectionary and snacking products. Brands like Pandy and True Co. saw high double-digit growth. I am particularly interested in this segment, and believe the products that they are currently producing are quite innovative. I am a regular customer of their Pandy products, which offers low-calorie candy which tastes very similar to regular candy but 1/3 of the calories. I am paying close attention to this segment going forward, since I believe this segment could witness very strong and stable growth going forward.

Quality nutrition saw sales decline 7% YoY due to customers postponing orders and also a slowdown for their manufacturing contracts services since Humble Group also produces goods for other companies. Lower demand caused lower utilisation which impacted profitability, EBIT margins going from 6.7% to 5.2% YoY.

Sustainable care's net sales increased 4% YoY, and gross margin has increased from 35.6% to 39.3% YoY, where the main cause consumer change towards higher quality and premium brands as Humble Co. and Naty. If this is an overall trend change, then that's a good sign for this segment going into the future.

Nordic Distribution which is their biggest segment saw a net sales increase of 6% YoY, which is good a sign of continued growth, however, gross margin contraction due to inventory sell out and combined with a weaker product mix saw margins fall for Nordic Distribution this quarter, and down more than 220 b.p. Yet, adjusted EBIT margin is up YoY because of operational efficiency measures that have been made for the group.

Growth

Humble Group is up TTM every quarter since Q1 2023, shows continuous growth. Humble Group's companies continue to produce new healthier products with unique features that do not compromise the taste of the products. The Nordic distributions have a wide range of different sectors that they distribute for, from household items, Scandinavian foods, European and Asian foods, this makes Humble diversified and less cyclical. Quality nutritions are having a break in growth, but the overall trend is there, and I believe more quality nutritions and health snacks is only getting popular and popular.

Sustainable care has continued momentum, and is another trend where consumers are looking for better and premium personal care products, and they are willing to pay a smaller premium for better solutions and products.

However, I believe Humble group's main growth will come from their future snacking segment. This segment does not only have the highest margins, but they also have the strongest growth by far. In this segment, they have managed to produce new, interesting products that consumers love. These products also have a higher price than their counterparts. Pandy candy for examples sell for almost twice the price per kilo as ordinary candy sold in Norway and Sweden. However, they have been smart with their packaging only selling 25 gram bags, so it seems cheap, and for some consumer that is just enough candy for a day.

If future snacking continues to grow double-digit the next five years, then I believe future snacking can be their biggest segment and also increase the group's EBIT margins closer to 6% since future snacking current EBIT margin is 8.5%. The current EBIT TTM 2025 is SEK 391 million. However, I believe future snacking itself will be able to achieve more than SEK 272 million EBIT by 2030. If everything else stays the same, we could expect an EPS of SEK 0.19. That is more than half of the current EPS of SEK 0.26, which shows the potential Humble group has and especially within this segment.

Risks

One of the reasons Humble Group has managed to grow so fast the past years is due to their big IPO in 2021 and that they also leveraged up at the same time. In 2021, their debt to equity ratio stood at 51%, but now has decreased down to 30%.

The existing credit facility agreement includes terms and conditions implying that the Net Interest Bearing Debt in relation to LTM Adjusted EBITDA Proforma excluding leasing must not exceed 3.25x and that the LTM Adjusted EBITDA in relation to Net Financial Expenses (as defined in the credit facility agreement) shall not be less than 4.00x at the end of this period. These terms and conditions have been met since the credit facility agreement was entered in the second quarter 2023.

We also intend to reduce our interest expenses going forward through quarterly amortisation of bank loans and increased profitability in absolute terms, where a reduced level of debt relative to EBITDA is expected to contribute to a lower financing cost for the Group.

Their current EBITDA to Net debt ratio is 2.8x, but their goal is to come closer to 2.5x because that will help reduce financing costs for the group.

I believe lower growth is Humble group's biggest challenge. If they don't manage to increase both profitability and top-line, then this is not the place to be invested.

Valuation and momentum

I have made a discounted EPS valuation based on three different scenarios to calculate the share price. All scenario's use the same EPS, which is SEK 0.4, and a discount rate of 10% which is my required rate of return and which I use for all my valuations.

The first scenario is the normal case, and it is based on an 8% growth rate and terminal multiple of 30x, which is today's current PE ratio. Calculates a share price of SEK 13.6 or $1.36.

The second scenario is the best case, it is based on a 10% growth rate, and a terminal multiple of 35x which is based on pre-historic valuations. Calculates a share price of 18 SEK, or $1.8 per share.

The third scenario is the worst case, it is based on a 3% growth rate, and a terminal multiple of 20x. This scenario shows how far down Humble group can go if their growth slows down. It calculates a share price of SEK 7 or $0.7.

Humble Group momentum not pointing in the right direction, the main ticker is down 33% on the Nasdaq Stockholm. The 1-month is up by a small amount of 3%, and the 3-month is still down considerably to -13%. This is definitely not a good time to invest.

Conclusion

Humble Group has had exceptional growth the past few years, but they have reached a more stable growth now. Some segments have struggled with either less growth or declining growth. However, the company is still growing 4% YoY, and I believe they will witness more growth going forward from their future snacking segment. This segment alone grew 19% YoY, and their ability to keep innovating and create interesting products is the reason they will keep growing. I believe this segment will be their biggest segment within some years, also increasing overall profitability for the group. Their other segments are witnessing slower growth, but the overall trend is pointing in the right direction. However, Humble Group's current momentum is bad due to slower overall growth and weaker EPS YoY, which is the reason I rate this stock a HOLD, but if I see momentum switching I will be quick to buy this stock whenever this happens!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
